subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — EOG Practice Test 1
instructions: Read each question carefully. Choose the best answer.

passages.title: The Last Tomato
passages.text:
Maya stood at the edge of the garden and frowned. All summer she had watered her tomato plant. She had pulled the weeds and chased away the hungry rabbits. Now there was only one red tomato left on the whole vine.

"I want to win the ribbon at the fair," Maya told her grandfather. "But one tomato is not enough. The Riveras down the street have a whole basket."

Grandpa knelt beside the plant. He touched the smooth red skin of the tomato. "Size and number are not everything," he said. "This little plant grew in poor, rocky soil. You gave it everything you had, and it gave you something back."

Maya was not so sure. At the fair the next morning, she set her single tomato on a small white plate. Next to it, the Riveras placed a mountain of shiny tomatoes. Maya wanted to hide.

The judge moved slowly down the table. She tasted the Riveras' tomatoes and nodded politely. Then she lifted Maya's tomato and took a careful bite. Her eyes opened wide. "This," she said, "is the sweetest tomato I have ever tasted."

Maya did not win the ribbon for the most tomatoes. But she won the blue ribbon for the best flavor. On the way home, she held the ribbon tightly and smiled at her grandfather. Now she understood what he had meant.

passages.title: How Bees Make Honey
passages.text:
Honey is a sweet food that many people love to eat. But honey does not come from a store. It comes from busy little insects called honeybees. Making honey takes thousands of bees and a great deal of work.

First, worker bees fly from flower to flower. They use a long tongue, shaped like a straw, to drink a sweet liquid called nectar. A bee stores the nectar in a special part of its body called the honey stomach. One bee may visit more than a hundred flowers before its honey stomach is full.

Next, the bee flies back to the hive. There it passes the nectar to other bees inside. The bees chew the nectar and pass it from bee to bee. This mixing changes the nectar and removes some of the water.

Then the bees spread the thick liquid into tiny wax rooms called cells. The bees flap their wings hard to fan the cells. The moving air dries the nectar even more until it becomes thick, golden honey. Finally, the bees cover each full cell with a cap of wax. The honey is now stored safely for the cold winter, when no flowers bloom.

Bees do not make honey for people. They make it to feed their own hive. But bees often make far more honey than they need. Beekeepers can take the extra honey without harming the bees. That is how the honey reaches our breakfast table.

passages.title: The Long Road of the Mail
passages.text:
Today a letter can travel across the country in just a few days. Long ago, sending a message was much harder. People in the United States had to wait weeks or even months for news from far away. Over time, clever ideas made the mail faster and faster.

In the early days, riders on horseback carried letters along bumpy dirt roads. One famous mail service was called the Pony Express. Riders raced from one station to the next, then jumped onto a fresh horse. By riding day and night, they carried mail across the West in about ten days. That was very fast for the time.

The Pony Express did not last long. A new invention called the telegraph could send messages over wires in only minutes. Soon railroads stretched across the land, and trains carried huge bags of mail much faster than any horse could run.

Later, airplanes carried the mail through the sky. A letter that once took weeks could now cross the whole country in a single day. Workers sorted millions of letters and packages so each one would reach the right house.

The way we send messages keeps changing. But the goal has always stayed the same. People want to share news, ideas, and kind words with those they love, no matter how far apart they are.

passages.title: The Old Oak Tree
passages.text:
In the corner of our yard
stands an oak both old and tall.
It has watched the seasons change
through the years, through them all.

In the spring its leaves unfold,
tiny, soft, and bright and green.
Birds come building little nests
in the branches, snug, unseen.

Summer brings a pool of shade
where I read and rest and dream.
The oak is like a kind old friend,
steady as a quiet stream.

Autumn paints the leaves with gold,
then they tumble, swirl, and fall.
Winter wraps the bare brown limbs
in a coat of snow for all.

Round and round the seasons go,
but the oak tree always stays.
Strong and patient, tall and proud,
it will stand for all my days.

questions.questionText: At the beginning of the story, why is Maya frowning?
options: Only one tomato is left on her plant.
Her grandfather will not help her.
Rabbits have eaten her whole garden.
She has lost her ribbon from the fair.
questions.explanation: The story says there was only one red tomato left on the whole vine, and that is why Maya frowns.

questions.questionText: What did Maya do all summer to care for her plant?
options: She watered it and pulled the weeds.
She moved it into the house.
She bought tomatoes from a store.
She gave it special plant food.
questions.explanation: The passage says Maya watered the plant, pulled the weeds, and chased away rabbits.

questions.questionText: Read this sentence from the story: "Next to it, the Riveras placed a mountain of shiny tomatoes." What does the word "mountain" mean here?
options: A very large pile
A tall, rocky hill
A kind of plant
A small white plate
questions.explanation: Here "mountain" is used to mean a very large pile of tomatoes, not a real hill.

questions.questionText: When Maya "wanted to hide" at the fair, how was she most likely feeling?
options: Embarrassed about having only one tomato
Excited to taste the other tomatoes
Angry at the judge for being slow
Sleepy after the long morning
questions.explanation: Her one tomato sat next to the Riveras' huge pile, so she felt embarrassed and wanted to hide.

questions.questionText: What is the main lesson, or theme, of this story?
options: Quality and care can matter more than how much you have.
You should always grow more plants than your neighbors.
Winning a ribbon is the only reason to enter a fair.
Rabbits will always ruin a garden in the summer.
questions.explanation: Maya's one tomato wins for flavor, showing that care and quality can matter more than number, just as Grandpa said.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the story BEST supports the idea that hard work paid off for Maya?
options: "But she won the blue ribbon for the best flavor."
"Maya stood at the edge of the garden and frowned."
"The Riveras down the street have a whole basket."
"Maya wanted to hide."
questions.explanation: Winning the blue ribbon for best flavor shows that all of Maya's care for the plant paid off.

questions.questionText: Why does the author include Grandpa's words, "Size and number are not everything"?
options: To hint at the lesson Maya will learn by the end
To explain how to grow tomatoes in rocky soil
To show that Grandpa wants to win the ribbon
To describe what the fair looks like
questions.explanation: Grandpa's words point ahead to the story's lesson, which Maya understands after her sweet tomato wins.

questions.questionText: Select all that apply. Which things did Maya do to protect or care for her tomato plant?
options: Watered the plant
Pulled the weeds
Chased away rabbits
Bought a basket of tomatoes
questions.explanation: The story says Maya watered the plant, pulled the weeds, and chased away the rabbits. The Riveras had a basket, not Maya.

questions.questionText: How many tomatoes were left on Maya's whole vine at the start of the story? Write the number.
questions.explanation: The story says there was only one red tomato left on the whole vine.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of "How Bees Make Honey"?
options: Honeybees do a lot of work to turn nectar into honey.
Honey is sold in stores all around the world.
Rabbits and bees both live near gardens.
Winter is the coldest season for honeybees.
questions.explanation: The whole article explains the steps bees take to change nectar into honey, so that is the main idea.

questions.questionText: What do bees drink from flowers?
options: Nectar
Water
Wax
Honey
questions.explanation: The article says bees use a long tongue to drink a sweet liquid called nectar.

questions.questionText: The article uses the words "First," "Next," "Then," and "Finally." How does the author organize the information?
options: In the order, or steps, that the bees follow
By comparing bees to other insects
By listing reasons people like honey
By describing problems and their solutions
questions.explanation: These order words show the article is organized as a sequence of steps the bees follow to make honey.

questions.questionText: Read this sentence: "They use a long tongue, shaped like a straw, to drink a sweet liquid." Why does the author compare the tongue to a straw?
options: To help the reader picture how the bee drinks
To explain that bees eat straw
To show that the tongue is hard and dry
To prove that bees are bigger than straws
questions.explanation: Comparing the tongue to a straw helps the reader picture how the bee sips up the nectar.

questions.questionText: Why do the bees store honey in the wax cells?
options: To have food during the cold winter when no flowers bloom
To share the honey with the beekeepers
To keep the rabbits out of the hive
To make the hive smell sweet
questions.explanation: The article says the honey is stored safely for the cold winter, when no flowers bloom.

questions.questionText: Why can beekeepers take honey from a hive without harming the bees?
options: Bees often make more honey than they need.
Bees do not eat honey at all.
Beekeepers give the bees nectar in return.
Bees make new honey every single day in winter.
questions.explanation: The article explains that bees often make far more honey than they need, so the extra can be taken safely.

questions.questionText: What is the author's main purpose for writing this article?
options: To inform readers about how bees make honey
To convince readers to become beekeepers
To tell a make-believe story about a bee
To entertain readers with a funny poem
questions.explanation: The article gives facts and steps to inform readers about how honey is made.

questions.questionText: According to the article, one bee may visit more than how many flowers before its honey stomach is full? Write the number.
questions.explanation: The article says one bee may visit more than a hundred (100) flowers before its honey stomach is full.

questions.questionText: Select all that apply. Which of these steps do the bees do to turn nectar into honey?
options: Chew the nectar and pass it bee to bee
Flap their wings to fan the cells
Cover each full cell with a wax cap
Buy more nectar from a store
questions.explanation: The bees chew and pass the nectar, fan the cells with their wings, and cap the cells with wax. Buying nectar is not in the article.

questions.questionText: What is this article mostly about?
options: How the mail has become faster over time
How to ride a horse on a dirt road
Why railroads are better than airplanes
How to write a friendly letter
questions.explanation: The article describes how new inventions made the mail faster and faster over time.

questions.questionText: How did riders in the Pony Express carry the mail?
options: On horseback, changing to fresh horses
On fast trains across the land
In airplanes through the sky
Over wires using the telegraph
questions.explanation: The article says Pony Express riders raced from station to station and jumped onto fresh horses.

questions.questionText: How is most of the information in this article organized?
options: In time order, from the past to the present
By comparing two countries
By listing the parts of a letter
From the most important idea to the least important
questions.explanation: The article moves through time, from horseback riders long ago to telegraphs, trains, and airplanes.

questions.questionText: The article says trains carried mail "much faster than any horse could run." The word "faster" helps show that trains were —
options: quicker than horses
older than horses
smaller than horses
louder than horses
questions.explanation: "Faster" compares speed, showing trains were quicker than horses.

questions.questionText: Why did the Pony Express "not last long"?
options: The telegraph could send messages much faster.
The horses got too tired to run.
People stopped sending any letters.
The dirt roads were closed by snow.
questions.explanation: Right after saying it did not last long, the article explains the telegraph could send messages in only minutes.

questions.questionText: According to the article, what stayed the same even as the way we send messages kept changing?
options: People's wish to share news with those they love
The number of horses on the dirt roads
The amount of time a letter takes to arrive
The kind of machines that carry the mail
questions.explanation: The last paragraph says the goal stayed the same: people want to share news, ideas, and kind words.

questions.questionText: Why did the author most likely write this article?
options: To explain how mail delivery changed and grew faster
To tell a scary story about a lost letter
To convince readers to ride the Pony Express
To teach readers how to build a railroad
questions.explanation: The author gives facts that explain how mail delivery changed and became faster over time.

questions.questionText: About how many days did it take the Pony Express to carry mail across the West? Write the number.
questions.explanation: The article says riders carried the mail across the West in about ten (10) days.

questions.questionText: Both "How Bees Make Honey" and "The Long Road of the Mail" are alike because both —
options: explain a process by giving facts in order
tell a make-believe story with talking animals
are written as poems with rhyming words
try to make the reader feel scared
questions.explanation: Both articles are informational and explain a process by giving facts in an order.

questions.questionText: Where does the old oak tree stand?
options: In the corner of the yard
Beside a quiet stream
Inside a snowy forest
Next to the garden gate
questions.explanation: The first line says the oak stands in the corner of "our yard."

questions.questionText: In which season do the birds build their nests in the tree?
options: Spring
Summer
Autumn
Winter
questions.explanation: The spring stanza says birds come building little nests in the branches.

questions.questionText: The poet writes, "The oak is like a kind old friend, / steady as a quiet stream." This comparison helps show that the tree is —
options: calm and dependable
loud and wild
small and weak
cold and lonely
questions.explanation: Comparing the oak to a kind friend and a quiet stream shows the tree is calm and dependable.

questions.questionText: In the line "then they tumble, swirl, and fall," what is "tumbling" and falling?
options: The leaves in autumn
The birds in spring
The snow in winter
The branches in summer
questions.explanation: The autumn stanza is about the gold leaves, which tumble, swirl, and fall.

questions.questionText: What is one main message of the poem?
options: The oak tree stays strong and steady as the seasons change.
Birds should not build nests in oak trees.
Winter is the best season of the year.
Trees grow faster when people read near them.
questions.explanation: Through every season the oak "always stays," strong and patient, which is the poem's message.

questions.questionText: How is the poem organized?
options: It moves through the four seasons of the year.
It lists reasons to plant a tree.
It tells about one single rainy day.
It compares an oak tree to a maple tree.
questions.explanation: The poem moves from spring to summer to autumn to winter, telling about the oak in each season.

questions.questionText: Select all that apply. Which words does the poet use to describe the oak tree?
options: Strong
Patient
Proud
Tiny
questions.explanation: The last stanza calls the oak "Strong and patient, tall and proud." "Tiny" describes the new spring leaves, not the whole tree.

questions.questionText: How does the speaker most likely feel about the oak tree?
options: The speaker loves and admires the tree.
The speaker is afraid of the tree.
The speaker wants to cut the tree down.
The speaker has never noticed the tree.
questions.explanation: The speaker calls the oak a kind old friend and rests under it, showing love and admiration.

questions.questionText: "The Old Oak Tree" is different from "How Bees Make Honey" because "The Old Oak Tree" —
options: is a poem with rhythm and rhyme
gives step-by-step facts only
explains how to make a food
lists the parts of an insect
questions.explanation: "The Old Oak Tree" is a poem with rhythm and rhyme, while the bee article gives step-by-step facts.
